data.title: Apple's AI Launch in China Key to 'Long Awaited Growth Catalyst' in Region, Wedbush Says
data.description:
Apple's potential launch of its Apple Intelligence in China is the "key to the long-awaited growth catalyst" in the region, Wedbush Securities said in a note Friday.

The firm said that based on its estimates, more than half of the roughly 200 million iPhones in China "are in the window of an upgrade opportunity" and Apple Intelligence, which was launched on iPhone 16, has been a catalyst for upgrades in the US.

Wedbush said China has been a "major headwind" for Apple, with the company losing some share to Huawei over the past year. Apple's deal with Alibaba , with Baidu also playing a role, to launch iPhone AI in China reportedly in early May, is expected to turn the tide, according to the note.

"We believe starting in the June quarter, growth returns to this flagship region with easier comps going forward into [fiscal year 2026]," Wedbush analysts, including Daniel Ives, said, adding that despite US-China tensions, they believe that Apple can balance its growth strategy in China as shown by the AI deal with Alibaba.

Wedbush maintained Apple's outperform rating and $325 price target.

data.title: Floor & Decor Holdings up after Telsey Advisory Group's upgrade
data.description:
** Shares of flooring supplies maker Floor & Decor Holdings rise as much as 4.7% to $102.49

** Brokerage Telsey Advisory Group upgrades stock to "outperform" from "market perform", raises PT by $25 to $115

** Believes housing turnover activity to grow as market is becoming conditioned to elevated mortgage rates

** However, sees lag between increased housing activity and flooring demand growth

** Expects company's sales to remain soft in short term but sees improved performance in 2025

** However, brokerage Wedbush downgrades stock to "neutral" from "outperform", cuts PT by $10 to $100

** Sees shallow 2025 demand recovery as company has no new product cycle and risk of mass deportations, tariffs; says stock has elevated valuation

** FND is expected to report Q4 results on February 20

** Seven of 26 brokerages rate stock "buy" or higher, 16 "hold" and three "sell"; with a median PT of $103.50- data compiled by LSEG

** FND fell ~10.6% in 2024

data.title: India slashes bourbon whisky tariffs amid intensifying Trump criticism
data.description:
India has slashed tariffs on bourbon whisky to 100% from 150%, a move that will benefit imports of brands like Suntory's Jim Beam, after U.S. President Donald Trump's criticised "unfair" levies in the South Asian market.

Trump, who met Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi at the White House this week, has railed against the climate for American businesses in India and unveiled a roadmap for reciprocal tariffs on countries that put duties on U.S. imports.

The tariff notification by the Indian government was dated Feb. 13 but gathered media attention only on Friday. It said the basic customs duty on bourbon will be 50%, with an additional levy of 50%, bringing the total to 100%.

Previously, such imports were taxed at 150%.

There will be no change on imports of other liquor products, which are also taxed at 150%.

The move would primarily benefit imports from the United States, said Pratik Jain, a partner at PwC India. "It signals that the Indian government is willing to explore changes in tariff items for strategic partner countries," he added.

Many foreign liquor companies such as Diageo and Pernod Ricard operate in India's $35 billion spirits market, and industry executives have often criticized India's high tariff regime for such imports.

Vinod Giri, director general at Brewers Association of India, said tariffs on bourbons, like motorbikes, had high optics value, adding the move was designed to reassure the U.S. of India's intent and to pre-empt any retaliatory action.

"As India seeks to integrate into global economy, there is an increasing acknowledgment that the import duties in India on alcohol are high and need be reduced in line with other duties," he said.

data.title: Informatica Shares Fall After Multiple Analyst Downgrades, Price Target Cuts Follow Q4 Results
data.description:
Informatica shares fell by more than 30% in recent Friday trading after multiple analyst downgrades followed the company's Q4 financial results announcement.

The company late Thursday posted lower year-over-year Q4 revenue that trailed FactSet analyst estimates.

RBC downgraded the software development company to sector perform from outperform and cut its price target to $19 from $35.

Wells Fargo downgraded Informatica to equal weight from overweight and lowered its price target to $19 from $34, while Baird changed its rating to neutral from outperform with a slashed price target of $19 from $35.

Deutsche Bank downgraded the company to hold from buy and slashed its price target to $21 from $36.

Other price target cuts included UBS and Truist Securities who lowered their targets to $19 from $30, and to $24 from $34, respectively.

data.title: Baltic index gains as rates rise across all vessel segments
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange's dry bulk sea freight index, which measures shipping rates for vessels transporting dry bulk commodities, rose on Friday, supported by higher rates across all vessel segments.

* The index , which factors in rates for capesize, panamax and supramax shipping vessels, rose by 12 points to 792 points. However, the index fell nearly 3% for the week.

* The capesize index (.BACI) gained 5 points to 716 points, while the index was down more than 14% during the week.

* Average daily earnings for capesize vessels (.BATCA), which typically transport 150,000-ton cargoes such as iron ore and coal, added $39 to $5,939.

* Singapore iron ore futures prices rose on Friday and were on track for a weekly gain, supported by heightened concerns over cyclone-led supply disruptions in major producer Australia.

* The panamax index (.BPNI) rose 6 points to 980 points, snapping a four-day losing streak. Meanwhile, the index dropped by over 5% during the week.

* Average daily earnings for panamax vessels (.BPWT), which usually carry 60,000-70,000 tons of coal or grain cargo, increased by $54 to $8,819.

* Among smaller vessels, the supramax index (.BSIS) was up 24 points at 765 points, posting gains for the ninth consecutive session. The index was up 13% for the week.

* U.S. President Donald Trump said earlier this week that he is going to impose reciprocal tariffs on imports but he did not specify the countries that will be affected by them.

data.title: South African rand firms after Trump's tariff move; local budget next week
data.description:
The South African rand strengthened on Friday after U.S. President Donald Trump said he planned to impose reciprocal tariffs on countries taxing U.S. imports, and as investors awaited a domestic budget next week.

At 1503 GMT, the rand traded at 18.32 against the dollar , about 0.9% stronger than its previous close.

Trump on Thursday tasked his economics team with planning for reciprocal tariffs on every country taxing U.S. imports, ramping up prospects for a global trade war.

Concerns of a trade war lifted gold prices, which were poised for a seventh consecutive weekly gain. The surge in prices of the safe-haven asset is supportive of the South African currency, ETM Analytics said in a research note.

ETM Analytics said the only major constraint to any directional move is South African Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana's budget speech next week and the guidance he will offer investors.

On the Johannesburg Stock Exchange, the blue-chip Top-40 index closed about 1.1% higher.

South Africa's benchmark 2030 government bond (ZAR2030=) was also stronger, with the yield down 6.5 basis points to 9.10%.

data.title: Zentek Subsidiary Albany Graphite Corp. Provides Update on Thermal Purification Testwork Results
data.description:
GUELPH, ON / ACCESS Newswire / February 14, 2025 / Zentek Ltd. ("Zentek" or the "Company") (TSX-V:ZEN), an intellectual property technology development and commercialization company is pleased to announce, further to its press release dated January 8, 2025, that its wholly owned subsidiary, Albany Graphite Corp. ("AGC") has received additional results from the fluidized bed reactor ("FBR") testwork. These results indicate that rare earth elements ("REE") and other elements of interest can be recovered as a by-product of the thermal purification process.

Highlights

  • Purification was performed utilizing an FBR directly on a ~950 g sample of the Albany flotation concentrate (85% Total Graphitic Carbon ("TGC")) with no intermediate hydrometallurgical steps and yielded 99.9991% purity

  • Impurity elements, including REEs, removed from the flotation concentrate during the purification process were collected and concentrated in the scrubber that handles all the FBR exhaust products

  • Zentek will continue to investigate the REE potential of the Albany graphite deposit and tailings

  • The Company also plans on exploring both production and processing of graphite to create a battery-ready anode active material and a cathode conductivity enhancement material, as well as ultra-high purity graphite for nuclear applications

As reported in Zentek's January 8, 2025, press release, the Company had contracted a manufacturing company specializing in industrial graphite and carbon to explore the direct purification of Albany flotation concentrate (~85% TGC) using its proprietary continuous processing equipment. Zentek supplied a one-kilogram sample of the homogenized bulk flotation concentrate, which was characterized, aggregated into 3-D particles, and then run in an electrothermal FBR. As part of the characterization process, an elemental analysis (59 elements) was performed to identify the impurity elements. In its report, the manufacturing company noted that the feed material contained concentrations of REEs and other potential elements of value that could be recovered as part of the thermal purification process. Impurity elements, including the REEs, that were removed from the graphite during the purification process were collected and concentrated in a scrubber that handles all the FBR exhaust products.

Next Steps

Zentek intends to continue to investigate the REE potential of the Albany graphite deposit and send samples of unprocessed Albany mineralization and tailings material for additional elemental analyses to verify their REE content and determine if there is any consistency within the two pipes, which REEs are enriched, and also provide an estimate of their average concentrations. Additional detailed sampling and verification would include analyzing the pulps and core from representative drill holes and the insertion of certified reference materials into the sample stream. It should be noted that the Mineral Resources and assay database do not currently include REE values and much detailed work is required before they can be considered.

Additionally, the ultra-high purity graphite will be evaluated for its suitability for both the lithium-ion battery and nuclear markets. This will require post-processing of thermally purified graphite and its testing in battery cells, as well as against the specifications of commercially available nuclear industry graphite material. Zentek has received quotations from the manufacturing company to complete this testwork.

Greg Fenton, CEO of Zentek and Albany Graphite Corp., commented: "This is an exciting development as we continue to gather information on the potential of the Albany project. The REEs and other elements that could be recovered during the purification process and potentially from the tailings could be a new by-product and help to offset the purification costs."

Mr. Peter Wood, P.Eng., P.Geo., Vice President, Development of AGC, a "Qualified Person" under NI 43-101, has reviewed the technical information contained in this news release.

Overview of the Albany Graphite Project

The Albany Graphite Project, an igneous-hosted fluid-derived graphite deposit located in Claim Block 4F, comprised of 521 mining claims (461 single-cell claims and 60 boundary-cell claims) is at an advanced exploration stage. The project is located west of the communities of Constance Lake First Nation ("CLFN") and Hearst, Ontario, within 30 km of the Trans-Canada Highway, close to established infrastructure including roads, rail, power transmission lines, and a natural gas pipeline.

About Zentek Ltd.

Zentek is an ISO 13485:2016 certified intellectual property technology company focused on the research, development and commercialization of novel products seeking to give the Company's commercial partners a competitive advantage by making their products better, safer, and greener.

Zentek's patented technology platform ZenGUARD™ is shown to have enhanced viral filtration efficiency for surgical masks and HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) systems. Zentek's ZenGUARD™ production facility is located in Guelph, Ontario

Zentek has a global exclusive license to the Aptamer-based platform technology developed by McMaster University which is being jointly developed Zentek and McMaster for both the diagnostic and therapeutic markets.

data.title: Google Unfazed by DeepSeek's AI Rise, Backs Billion-Dollar Strategy
data.description:
Google (GOOG, GOOGL) remains confident in its AI dominance despite the emergence of China's DeepSeek, according to comments from DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis during a company meeting in Paris on Wednesday, as reported by CNBC.

Addressing employee concerns, Hassabis dismissed worries over DeepSeek's advancements, claiming that some of its AI-related claims are exaggerated. He suggested that DeepSeek underreported the true costs and hardware requirements for training its AI and may have relied on Western AI models. Still, he acknowledged DeepSeek's potential, calling it "the best team I've seen come out of China" and warning of possible security and geopolitical implications.

DeepSeek made headlines in late January when it unveiled its AI model R1, boasting that large language models (LLMs) could be built on a tight budget and without human supervision. The news sparked a major selloff in tech stocks, with Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) losing $589 billion in market value on January 27. Despite DeepSeek's cost-saving approach, U.S. tech giantsincluding Google, Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT)remain committed to heavy AI investments.

They spent billions in their last quarters and plan to continue in 2025, favoring a scalability-focused AI strategy over DeepSeek's frugal model. Meanwhile, Google executives also addressed concerns over changes to the company's AI Principles, specifically the removal of its prior pledge to not use AI for weapons or surveillance. Google's President of Global Affairs Kent Walker defended the update, explaining that AI has evolved into "more nuanced scenarios" since the original guidelines were set in 2018.

data.title: Timken Declares Quarterly Dividend of 34 Cents Per Share
data.description:
Timken Declares Quarterly Dividend of 34 Cents Per Share

PR Newswire

NORTH CANTON, Ohio, Feb. 14, 2025

NORTH CANTON, Ohio, Feb. 14, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — The board of directors of The Timken Company (; www.timken.com), a global technology leader in engineered bearings and industrial motion, today declared a quarterly cash dividend of 34 cents per share. The dividend is payable on March 7, 2025, to shareholders of record as of Feb. 25, 2025.

Timken has paid a dividend on its common shares every quarter since its original listing on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) in 1922. The upcoming dividend represents 411 consecutive quarters, one of the longest-running dividend streaks among NYSE-listed companies.

About The Timken Company

The Timken Company (; www.timken.com), a global technology leader in engineered bearings and industrial motion, designs a growing portfolio of next-generation products for diverse industries. For more than 125 years, Timken has used its specialized expertise to innovate and create customer-centric solutions that increase reliability and efficiency. Timken posted $4.6 billion in sales in 2024 and employs approximately 19,000 people globally, operating from 45 countries.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1015 ET - Weak economic growth in the eurozone could result in poor performance in risk assets and equities, analysts at Pimco say in a note. "There is reason to remain cautious given the challenging macroeconomic outlook and what we view as complacent valuations," they say. The euro is expected to depreciate against the dollar given the economic challenges and threats of U.S. tariffs, Pimco says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1011 ET - The current uncertain environment provides a favorable landscape for bonds, says Pimco's Nicola Mai in a note. "With current yield levels significantly higher than those before the pandemic, there are attractive opportunities for fixed income investors," the economist and sovereign credit analyst says. European duration is attractive, particularly in the 5- and 10-year part of the curve, he says in a note. Pimco tends to be underweight the long end of the curve due to increased sovereign issuance and reduced central bank support. "Having duration exposure in portfolios also offers significant upside in the event of more adverse global trade scenarios," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1005 ET - Two Canadian market strategists believe tariff exhaustion is settling in among traders, with President Trump's threats of hefty, broad tariffs failing to materialize. "Investors seem to be smelling a rat on this tariff file," says David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research, citing the 25% tariff reprieve for Canada and Mexico, and Thursday's announcement about reciprocal tariffs that produced no immediate action. Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at forex firm Corpay, says that rightly or wrongly, fears of a global trade war are easing, citing declining 10-year Treasury yields and buoyant stock markets. Schamotta adds, though, that uncertainty is likely to remain elevated because "observers, including ourselves, are lacking a clear understanding of Trump's motivations." (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0941 ET - Big-ticket spending fell at U.S. retailers last month, a sign that consumers' instincts to make big purchases before new tariffs land is already fading, economists at Pantheon Macro write. Furniture sales were down by 1.7% last month and spending at electronics and appliance stores dropped by 0.7%. "We had thought that pre-emptive purchases had further to run," Pantheon writes, "but this report seems to suggest that this boost to spending is already fading." Comparing nominal retail-spending growth against inflation numbers suggests that consumer spending fell in January in inflation-adjusted terms, a poor omen for 1Q GDP growth overall, Pantheon writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0933 ET - A sharp fall in Canadian manufacturing sales volumes in December suggests the earlier recovery for the sector has hit a wall, Capital Economics' Thomas Ryan reckons. A 0.3% rise in nominal sales on-month was weaker than the flash estimate for a 0.6% advance. In volume terms, sales were down 0.8%. Ryan says that with inventories little changed, manufacturing GDP appears to have fallen. And that contrasts with Statistics Canada's earlier estimate that industry-level GDP rose 0.2% on-month with a positive contribution from manufacturing, as well as retail trade and construction. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0916 ET - The 0.9% decline in retail sales last month suggests that broader economic growth is decelerating, economists at Capital Economics write. They grant that cold weather may have temporarily dampened consumer spending last month, but Capital notes that the decline was broad based, hitting categories like sporting goods and hobby stores, furniture, and electronics and appliances. The decline of 2.8% at car and car-parts dealers was especially sharp, but even excluding that category, retail sales were lower by 0.4%. The "suggests that GDP growth will slow to less than 2% annualized this quarter," Capital writes, from 2.3% in 4Q. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

0905 ET - Global bonds recorded their 57th consecutive week of net inflows, totaling $13.7 billion, TD Securities says in a note. The drive comes from developed markets, while emerging markets saw net outflows of $0.15 billion, it says. U.S. Treasurys saw $13.7 billion of inflows, accounting for the bulk, followed by western Europe with $1.1 billion, TD Securities says. Outflows were led by global and U.K. bond funds with $1.5 billion and $0.9 billion, respectively. Within U.S. fixed income, mixed government and credit funds led with $4.4 billion in inflows, which have also amassed the highest inflows over the past three months, totaling $32.3 billion. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0900 ET - The Romanian leu continues to trade steady after Romania's central bank left interest rates unchanged at 6.5% as widely anticipated by analysts. The central bank said it expects inflation to fall in the second half but on a higher path than previously expected, staying above the variation band of the target until year-end. "In our view, inflation won't return to the central bank's target range until mid-2026," Capital Economics economist Nicholas Farr says in a note. Capital Economics forecasts 50 basis points of rate cuts in the second half to 6.0% but "risks are tilted toward a more delayed restart of the easing cycle." EUR/RON trades at 4.9771, little changed after the decision. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0859 ET - U.S. credit spreads have remained resilient, despite increased volatility in Treasury yields due to uncertainty around U.S. interest rate cuts and tariffs, Barclays analysts say in a note. "Despite the preponderance of policy-related headlines, credit markets have remained calm and well anchored near recent tights," they say. The resilient performance has been driven by strong investor demand and healthy corporate balance sheets, Barclays says. "Persistent yield-driven demand and still-intact fundamentals continue to underpin credit market stability." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0858 ET - A resolution to the Russia-Ukraine war by U.S. President Trump could be a boon for Europe, likely resulting in a joint EU issuance fund for reconstruction and development, Nomura analysts say. While real money clients cannot invest in bonds allocated to defence spending, they can invest in bonds allocated to reconstruction, the analysts say in a note. "Therefore, we could see a significant increase in joint EU issuance in 2025, which we believe would be well-received by investors," the analysts say. Nomura would also expect the bulk of winning tenders for rebuilding Ukraine to be European companies. "This would be a boon for European construction and manufacturing sectors, both of which have been languishing since the 2022 gas crisis." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0854 ET - TD Securities scales back its call for interest rate cuts from the Bank of Canada, citing improving economic data. The firm now anticipates two more BOC reductions, to 2.5%, as opposed to its earlier call for three cuts to 2.25%. BOC is "caught in a conundrum, trying to balance the impacts of trade uncertainty against a decent run of economic data," TD tells clients. TD's call assumes Canada escapes broad, hefty tariffs from the Trump administration, such as a 25% levy on all nonenergy goods. The firm adds that, under this scenario, two-year government of Canada bonds are more likely to sell-off than rally from current levels. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0852 ET - Treasury yields and the dollar extend early losses as January retail sales fall more than expected. Overall sales shrink 0.9%, while economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast a 0.2% contraction. December's expansion is revised to 0.7% from 0.4%. The ex-auto print was a 0.4% decline, versus consensus of a 0.3% expansion. January industrial production is due at 9:15 a.m. ET. Yields were already weak following yesterday's PPI inflation report. The 10-year is at 4.484% and the two-year at 4.274%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.3%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1015 ET - Weak economic growth in the eurozone could result in poor performance in risk assets and equities, analysts at Pimco say in a note. "There is reason to remain cautious given the challenging macroeconomic outlook and what we view as complacent valuations," they say. The euro is expected to depreciate against the dollar given the economic challenges and threats of U.S. tariffs, Pimco says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1014 ET - Ahead of next week's slated results from Walmart, Bank of America again raises its price objective on shares of the retail behemoth. In a note, BofA's Robert Ohmes and Kendall Toscano say they expect Walmart's 4Q same-store sales will continue to reflect positive year-over-year transactions and strength in grocery and health/wellness. BofA says Walmart continues to gain share across product categories and incomes, and the analysts see improving long-term profitability supported by growth in high-margin digital advertising and third-party marketplace, and reductions in core e-commerce losses. BofA, with a buy rating on Walmart, raises its price objective to $120 from $110 after lifting it from $105 last month. Walmart down 0.5% to $104.55. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

1002 ET - TreeHouse Foods says the industry is going through dramatic changes and uncertainty, thanks to the rise of weight-loss drugs, inflation and more. CEO Steve Oakland says the store-brand food company makes goods that have seen annual sales growth of 3% to 5% by volume in the past decade, versus today's low- to no-growth environment. Oakland says the impact of weight-loss drugs will become clearer over the next year or two and that now is a good time for TreeHouse to rethink how it runs its business. "We can't have a more uncertain environment today with tariffs and all the things that are going on around us," Oakland says. He adds that the company is "making strategic decisions on margin management on new and existing business with a goal of prioritizing gross profit dollars." TreeHouse surges 18%. (jesse.newman@wsj.com)

0928 ET - China is becoming more important for Magna International, both from a sales perspective and in terms of vehicle production. In 2025, light vehicle production from China is expected to reach 29.7 million, and then to grow to 30.8 million in 2026. This compares with North America reaching 15.1 million and 15.4 million, respectively for 2025 and 2026, and 16.6 million and 17 million in Europe. On an analyst call, executives say that sales from China are expected to be around $5.6 billion in 2025 and to reach "north of $6 billion by 2026." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0913 ET - Starbucks is expanding further in the Middle East even as sales in the region have suffered following a boycott in response to the war between Israel and Hamas. The coffee chain plans to open about 500 new stores in the next five years in the Middle East, according to an interview that Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol gave to Bloomberg TV. Niccol said information around the boycott was false and that Starbucks has never supported any militaries. "We're focused on what we know to be true. We're focused on growing our brand around our mission," Niccol said. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0848 ET - Magna International expects a number of headwinds to weigh on 2025 profit before rebounding modestly in 2026. On an analyst call, executives say FX headwinds will be one of the main drags due largely to a stronger US dollar, as well as a less favorable program mix, and the end of production of Jaguar's assembly programs. Generally, globally lower light vehicle production will be the overall drag. However, management sees a pick up in 2026, and sales are expected between $40.5 billion and $42.6 billion, up from an $38.6 billion to $40.2 billion expected in 2025. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0840 ET - TD Cowen says Magna's 2025 guidance is well below consensus, which implies "sales at the mid-point to decline about 8% year-over-year to $39.4 billion," compared with consensus of $42.8 billion. Analyst Brian Morrison calls the outlook for the auto parts manufacturer "not surprisingly--soft," and also notes in a report that the EBIT margin guidance range of 5.3%-5.8% implies mid-point 2025 adjusted EBITDA and EPS of around $3.71 billion and $5.04, respectively, compared with consensus of $4 billion and $5.84. Tariffs could be another drag, but the company says its outlook does not incorporate any potential impact of the imposition of tariffs or changes in tariff rates. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0837 ET - "Trade wars and punitive tariffs make no sense," European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen says in a speech at the Munich Security Conference. She says that unjustified tariffs on the European Union would be met with a response, and cautions that these could disrupt key transatlantic supply chains, drive inflation and harm workers, middle-income families and businesses on both sides of the atlantic. The EU faces "a clear attempt by some to build spheres of influence" and a "more transactional approach in global affairs," the chief of the bloc's executive arm says. Europe must not underestimate the "disruptive potential of intense competition or even a bipolar conflict between China and the United States," von der Leyen adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0816 ET - TC Energy's 4Q performance at first blush looks to be neutral, with results coming in line with consensus expectations and guidance for 2025 unchanged, Jefferies' Anthony Linton says. Adjusted Ebitda was C$2.6 billion, as a lower-than-expected contribution from TC's natural gas pipelines was offset by a higher contribution from the power business thanks to a strong performance at the Bruce nuclear facility. Alongside the results, the company announced a 3.3% rise in its quarterly dividend, implying an annual yield of about 5.1% and in line with 2025 forecasts, Linton says. Jefferies has a C$71 target on the shares, which last closed at C$67.20. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0758 ET - European airlines are likely to benefit from a Ukraine-Russia ceasefire, if the deal leads to the reopening of Russian airspace to overflights for European carriers, JP Morgan analyst Harry Gowers says. A ceasefire could also cause downward pressure on jet fuel prices, improved air traffic control, and tighter European capacity, he writes in a research note. However, it would initially put pressure on air freight rates, harming logistics companies, he says. The outcomes for Wizz Air and Lufthansa would range from neutral to very positive, Gowers adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0750 ET - A Ukraine-Russia ceasefire could deliver an increase in the Eurozone's gross domestic product of 0.2% if there is a limited deal, and 0.5% in an upside scenario, Goldman Sachs analysts say. An agreement to halt the conflict would lower risk premium and energy prices, and boost consumer confidence and economic growth, they say. But Goldman Sachs cautions that the war has revealed Europe's structural problems, such as energy dependence, and the continent appears to have been excluded from the current proposed negotiations--arguably a sign of Europe's weakened geopolitical position. The analysts write that there is room for a modest additional re-rating in European stocks, noting that Europe's STOXX 600 has underperformed S&P 500 and MSCI World by 13% and 6% respectively since Russia launched its large-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0738 ET - NATO members must commit to a "strong timetable" to meet a higher defense spending target when they meet in the Hague for the alliance's annual summit on June 24-25, NATO's Secretary General Mark Rutte tells reporters on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference. NATO countries must avoid the mistake of 2014, when they agreed to increase spending to 2% of their gross domestic product, but didn't set a timetable, leaving significant increases on hold until Donald Trump became the U.S. President for the first time in 2017, Rutte says. Trump recently called on NATO allies to spend 5% of their GDP, a figure that none of the 32 alliance members currently meets, not even the U.S. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

"This would be a boon for European construction and manufacturing sectors, both of which have been languishing since the 2022 gas crisis." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0854 ET - TD Securities scales back its call for interest rate cuts from the Bank of Canada, citing improving economic data. The firm now anticipates two more BOC reductions, to 2.5%, as opposed to its earlier call for three cuts to 2.25%. BOC is "caught in a conundrum, trying to balance the impacts of trade uncertainty against a decent run of economic data," TD tells clients. TD's call assumes Canada escapes broad, hefty tariffs from the Trump administration, such as a 25% levy on all nonenergy goods. The firm adds that, under this scenario, two-year government of Canada bonds are more likely to sell-off than rally from current levels. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0852 ET - Treasury yields and the dollar extend early losses as January retail sales fall more than expected. Overall sales shrink 0.9%, while economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast a 0.2% contraction. December's expansion is revised to 0.7% from 0.4%. The ex-auto print was a 0.4% decline, versus consensus of a 0.3% expansion. January industrial production is due at 9:15 a.m. ET. Yields were already weak following yesterday's PPI inflation report. The 10-year is at 4.484% and the two-year at 4.274%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.3%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Acquisition strengthens Trelleborg’s position in pipe repair in North America
data.description:
Trelleborg Group has, through its Trelleborg Industrial Solutions business area, signed an agreement to acquire the US-based pipe repair specialists NuFlow Technologies.

NuFlow, headquartered in Escondido, California, produces specialist liners, resins, and equipment for small-diameter pipe repair. Its manufacturing operations are in Ajax, Ontario, Canada. The business offers repair solutions for lateral and building interior pipes for residential and commercial segments. Sales in 2024 amounted to approximately SEK 180 M.

“We are delighted to welcome NuFlow to Trelleborg. This acquisition will allow us to better support customers through an enhanced local presence while the addition of interior pipe repair capability brings a new and exciting element to our already strong offering,” says Jean-Paul Mindermann, Business Area President of Trelleborg Industrial Solutions.

In May 2024, Trelleborg acquired the Finnish pipe repair specialist Boldan, strengthening the business’s position in the Nordic region. Bolt-on acquisitions, combined with investments in existing operations, align with the Group’s strategy to increase exposure to water infrastructure. This industry is set for significant growth as utility providers and local authorities address neglected critical infrastructure in the coming years.

The transaction is expected to be finalized in the first quarter of 2025.

data.title: Emaar Properties Logs Higher FY24 Attributable Profit, Revenue
data.description:
Emaar Properties (DFM:EMAAR) said Friday net attributable profit and revenue for 2024 grew year over year.

Net profit attributable to owners of the parent for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 13.51 billion Emirati dirhams, compared with 11.63 billion dirhams earlier. EPS moved to 1.53 dirhams from 1.32 dirhams.

The Dubai-listed real estate developer's revenue was 35.50 billion dirhams, compared with 26.75 billion dirhams a year ago.

The board intends to declare a dividend of 1 dirham per share for 2024. It paid 0.50 dirham per share in 2023.

data.title: DHL Owner Deutsche Post to Face Further Strikes Over Wage Disputes
data.description:
By Nina Kienle

DHL owner Deutsche Post said that negotiations with trade union Ver.di on wage disputes have failed.

The German logistics group said Friday that it made an offer of a 3.8% payment increase and an additional day of vacation in the third round of negotiations.

Ver.di in a separate statement announced further strikes. These won't take place until Germany's federal election on Feb. 23, in order to ensure that postal voting runs smoothly, it said.

A date for a fourth round of negotiations hasn't been set, the trade union confirmed.

Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com

data.title: Wall St subdued as markets await tariff details
data.description:
Key points:
  • Airbnb rises after upbeat quarterly results
  • DaVita falls after Q4 results; Berkshire Hathaway cuts stake
  • January retail sales at -0.9% MoM vs -0.1% estimate
  • Indexes: Dow unchanged, S&P 500 up 0.08%, Nasdaq up 0.02%

Wall Street's main indexes were muted on Friday, as investors awaited more clarity on U.S. President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariff plans after robust gains in the last sessions, with all three benchmarks set for weekly gains.

Trump tasked his economics team on Thursday with devising plans for reciprocal tariffs on every country taxing U.S. imports, though the directive stopped short of imposing fresh tariffs.

Howard Lutnick, Trump's pick for commerce secretary, said the administration would address each affected country one by one and said studies on the issue would be completed by April 1.

"The tariff news created a lot of volatility about two weeks ago, but right now it seems that the markets are looking past it," said Larry Tentarelli, chief technical strategist and founder of Blue Chip Daily Trend Report.

"The markets probably see the tariffs as more of a negotiating tool than anything else."

Imposition of tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, a bigger-than-expected rise in January's consumer prices and hawkish comments from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell added to market volatility this week, with uncertainty likely to persist.

However, all three major indexes are set for robust weekly gains, with the S&P 500 currently just shy of its record high hit three weeks ago.

The S&P 500 closed about 1% higher on Thursday, boosted by gains in Nvidia , Apple and Tesla .

Stocks also got a boost after data showed U.S. producer prices increased in January, while key elements in the core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) index, a measure closely tracked by the Fed, were benign or lower.

Traders are fully pricing in at least one 25-basis-point interest rate cut by the end of the year, with an about 50% chance of an additional such reduction, as per data complied by LSEG.

At 9:40 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was unchanged at 44,711.17. The S&P 500 gained 4.61 points, or 0.08%, to 6,119.68 and the Nasdaq Composite inched 4.47 points, or 0.02%, higher to 19,950.12.

Nine of the 11 S&P 500 sectors traded higher, with energy leading gains with a 1.5% rise, tracking rising oil prices.

Meanwhile, U.S. retail sales fell more than expected in January, dropping 0.9% last month after an upwardly revised 0.7% increase in December.

Yields across U.S. government bonds ticked lower after the data, with the one on the 10-year note last at 4.47%.

Nvidia outpaced most megacap and growth stocks, adding 1.4%.

Airbnb jumped 12.3% after the vacation home rentals company posted higher quarterly revenue.

DaVita dropped 14.7% after the dialysis firm projected annual profit below estimates. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway also sold some of its shares in the company.

U.S. markets will remain closed on Monday for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 2.91-to-1 ratio on the NYSE and by a 1.64-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted 23 new 52-week highs and two new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 81 new highs and 20 new lows.
